claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances.

Let me start with a few questions:

**Do you have any children that you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child I'm asking about - my son Alex. He's 19 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you're asking about your son Alex, who is 19 years old and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is 19, his eligibility depends on whether he's in approved education or training. Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 if they're in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, vocational courses, or similar)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you! Since Alex is 19 and studying for his A-levels, that would be considered approved education.

Now I need to ask you a few more questions to complete the eligibility assessment:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One more important question:

**Is Alex working for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working that many hours - he's focused on his studies. And he doesn't claim any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ Alex is 19 years old and studying for his A-levels, which counts as approved education
✓ You can get Child Benefit for children under 20 who are in approved education or training
✓ Alex lives at home with you in the UK
✓ He's not working 24+ hours per week
✓ He's not claiming benefits in his own right
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

**Important note:** Please be aware that if either you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. However, this doesn't affect your eligibility to claim - it just means you might need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return.

You can proceed with your Child Benefit claim. If you haven't already claimed, you can do so through the official government process.

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and currently studying for his A-levels, which counts as approved education. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives at home with the claimant in the UK, meaning the claimant is responsible for him. Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week, and he is not claiming benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance), both of which would disqualify him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, all eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 19 years old. Alex is currently in approved education (studying for A-levels), lives at home, is not working 24+ hours per week, and is not claiming benefits in his own right. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.
